romania.title: Au împreună 316 ani și i-au uimit pe cercetători. Secretul a trei surori din Brazilia care au trecut de 100 de ani
romania.summary: Trei surori din Brazilia, care au împreună 316 ani și au fost desemnate recent de Guinness World Records drept cel mai vârstnic trio de surori din lume, se află în atenția cercetătorilor care studiază mecanismele longevității. Oamenii de știință speră că povestea lor de viață și factorii genetici ar putea oferi indicii importante despre secretul unei vieți de peste un secol, relatează Reuters.

romania.title: Filmul De capul nostru, regizat de Tudor Cristian Jurgiu și premiat la Berlinale, intră în cinematografele din România din 26 iunie

medical.title: AI framework helps identify new targets for CAR T cell therapy
medical.summary: Leading CAR T cell therapy researchers have developed a human-in-the-loop artificial intelligence (AI) framework that centers scientists' expertise to find viable target antigens for CAR T cell therapy. The work was led by experts from the Perelman School of Medicine at the University of Pennsylvania and Penn's Abramson Cancer Center and has been published in Cell.

medical.title: Language models read doctors' notes to reveal why patients discontinue medication
medical.summary: Researchers at the University of Tartu showed that large language models can identify with high accuracy why patients stop using antidiabetic medications or statins based on doctors' electronic clinical notes. The study opens new possibilities for using clinical information that has so far been difficult to analyze in health research.

medical.title: Hidden thymus step may prime cancer-killing T cells earlier than expected
medical.summary: Researchers at Mayo Clinic have uncovered a previously hidden step in how the immune system prepares to fight cancer, a discovery that could help scientists develop more effective and longer-lasting cancer immunotherapies.

medical.title: Use of supplement before surgery may improve liver regeneration
medical.summary: Taking a supplement before a hepatectomy, a surgery in which part of the liver is removed, may help the organ recover more quickly and become more resistant to further damage. This is the main finding of a study conducted by researchers at the State University of Campinas (UNICAMP) in the state of São Paulo, Brazil. The researchers investigated the effects of beta-hydroxy-beta-methylbutyrate (HMB) in an experimental model using mice. The results indicate that preoperative use of the supplement preserves the energy function of liver cells and improves the quality of regeneration following major injury. These results were published in March in the journal Acta Physiologica.

medical.title: Not everyone has equal access to pain rehabilitation
medical.summary: Chronic pain can affect work, everyday life and quality of life. Patients with chronic pain do not have access to specialized rehabilitation on equal terms. Education, age, sex and region of birth appear to play a role, particularly in combination, according to a study from the University of Gothenburg published in the Scandinavian Journal of Pain.

medical.title: Developing a new methodology to identify biomarkers for male infertility
medical.summary: Researchers from the EHU-University of the Basque Country have developed a robust protocol to detect the molecules involved in the metabolic reactions of sperm. The new method enables a detailed analysis even with small samples and allows the detection of the largest number of potential biomarkers identified to date; this advance could help diagnose male infertility and develop future clinical solutions. The paper is published in the journal Cells.

medical.title: First use of precision editing to study human embryo development reveals role of master gene
medical.summary: Research led by the University of Cambridge Loke Center for Trophoblast Research has shown that a genome-editing technique can be used to alter a single gene in human embryonic cells, enabling the study of very early human development in unparalleled detail. The study is published in the journal Nature.

medical.title: This common vitamin deficiency can mimic normal aging
medical.summary: Vitamin B12 is needed in microscopic amounts, but a shortage can have major effects on health and energy. The vitamin was first linked to a lifesaving liver treatment for pernicious anemia nearly 100 years ago. Today, researchers are finding that B12 may also help keep cellular powerhouses called mitochondria functioning properly. This could explain why some people experience fatigue and brain fog even before traditional signs of deficiency show up.

medical.title: More can be done to reduce Legionnaires' disease risk in Australia, according to study
medical.summary: Flinders University researchers have identified opportunities to strengthen Legionella risk management across Australia by introducing uniform best practices across all states and territories.

medical.title: Poor metabolic health can age the brain even in young people, finds new large-scale study
medical.summary: Two people of very different ages can have a similar level of biological aging in their brains. Such an occurrence is possible because aging and metabolic health follow two distinct pathways that influence brain health. While it is known that the brain changes as we get older, a recent study analyzing more than 3,000 brain scans found that metabolic issues affect the brain through a different biological pathway than aging does.

medical.title: Radioligand therapy for HER2-positive cancers enters human trials for the first time
medical.summary: The McGill University Health Centre (MUHC) is the first site in the world to launch a first-in-human phase 1 clinical trial evaluating a novel radioligand therapy for HER2-positive cancers, including certain types of breast cancer.

medical.title: Scientists discover how a single cell builds a brain with 170 billion cells
medical.summary: How does a single cell build a brain with billions of precisely organized neurons? Researchers suggest that brain cells use their lineage—their cellular family tree—as a kind of positional map. Cells that come from the same ancestor stay near one another, helping the brain organize itself without relying solely on chemical signals.

medical.title: Targeting cannabinoid receptors in bladder sensory pathways may alleviate pain of cystitis
medical.summary: The burning pain associated with cystitis affects millions of people around the world, and it can take days or even years for some patients to find relief from bladder pain, infection-related symptoms and the constant need to urinate. A new study led by Flinders University published in Autonomic Neuroscience, has shown how certain pain receptors in the bladder could be targeted to reduce the debilitating effects of this common condition.

medical.title: Unfolding stomach capsule could target ulcer-causing bacteria for 48 hours
medical.summary: Between 50% and 100% of people in countries with poor access to clean water are infected with a bacterium called Helicobacter pylori. This bacterium is a leading cause of stomach ulcers, and it can even cause gastric cancer.

medical.title: They knew the pill was fake but their memory still improved
medical.summary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.

science.title: Artificial 'leaf' powers wireless biomedical device
science.summary: Plants convert light into energy efficiently through photosynthesis—an ability that scientists and engineers still struggle to match with electronic devices. Recently, researchers have looked beyond traditional semiconductor materials to create devices using a promising class of materials called nanoplasmonics. These tiny metal structures can absorb and concentrate optical energy and generate energetic charge carriers.

science.title: New study calls for rethink of domestic abuse responses in children's services
science.summary: A major new study has raised concerns about how domestic violence and abuse is understood and responded to within children's services.

science.title: Heat-struck Italians cool off in ancient stone 'trulli'
science.summary: Once relics of a bygone way of life, the iconic dry-stone dwellings with conical roofs dotted across Puglia are in increasing demand as cool havens in Italy's sweltering south.

science.title: Hybrid reef project off Miami Beach targets wave attenuation and coral recovery
science.summary: University of Miami researchers and collaborators have expanded an offshore reef restoration and coastal resilience project off Miami Beach with the deployment of three 3D-printed SEAHIVE clustered structures.

science.title: Sponges may cut methylmercury contamination in marine food webs by more than 50%
science.summary: Marine sponges may play an important, previously underestimated role in reducing methylmercury contamination in marine food webs. In a new modeling study, researchers at Helmholtz-Zentrum Hereon showed that sponges can significantly influence the spread of this environmental toxin through ecosystems. The findings suggest that the animals' unique feeding behavior helps reduce methylmercury levels and may lower contamination in fish. The study is published in the journal Biogeosciences.

science.title: Study explores environmental cost, perceptions of influencer PR gifts
science.summary: As part of their marketing strategy, many companies send public relations packages full of curated products to social media influencers, who film themselves opening the gifts on platforms like Instagram and TikTok.

science.title: Taking a step back changes group discussions in virtual environments
science.summary: In group decision-making, it is critical for each member to engage in discussions with a broader perspective and without fixating on personal values and knowledge. While self-distancing, or stepping back and viewing oneself from a third-person perspective, has been associated with more objective thinking, its role in multiparty decision-making within virtual environments remains underexplored.

science.title: Trios of quantum particles form checkerboard layouts when particle density hits sweet spot
science.summary: Trions form when three particles, like quarks or electrons, come together. This formation occurs in quantum particles in nuclear physics, semiconductors and magnets, and understanding its behavior can be challenging. Rice University's Kaden Hazzard and his team recently developed a theory on how these formations occur and behave, which was published in Physical Review Letters.

science.title: Researchers identify dual-function rice gene that boosts drought tolerance and grain yield
science.summary: As climate change intensifies droughts and other environmental stresses, maintaining crop productivity has become a major challenge for global agriculture. Drought can impair chloroplast development, reducing photosynthetic efficiency and ultimately lowering crop yields.

science.title: What really controls water chemistry in nanoscale spaces
science.summary: Water is the most studied molecule on Earth, yet a surprisingly basic question has gone unanswered for decades: When water is squeezed into gaps just a few molecules wide—as happens inside nanoscale pores, membranes and biological channels—does it become more or less chemically reactive?

science.title: Study analyzes how genes and education influence socioeconomic success
science.summary: Ambitious public education policies have the capacity to cushion the role of the "genetic lottery" in people's life chances. This is one of the main conclusions of a scientific study by Universidad Carlos III de Madrid (UC3M), the University of Lausanne (Switzerland) and Stockholm University (Sweden) analyzing the impact of an educational reform in England. These findings could be applied to the design of future educational policies.

science.title: Thirsty desert lizards inspire a new water-harvesting system
science.summary: When the desert horned lizard (Phrynosoma platyrhinos) is thirsty, it cannot just lap up water or scoop it up like a bird because it lives in environments where water is extremely scarce. Typically, it's found in damp soil or, even more rarely, in drops of rain.

science.title: Megacluster of bacterial genes reveals four antibiotics that jointly starve rivals of biotin
science.summary: Researchers at McMaster University have discovered what they describe as a "megacluster" of genes in Streptomyces bacteria that produces four antibiotics that work together to stop rival bacteria.

science.title: Semi-communal 'compound houses' in Ghana affect how people participate in political activity
science.summary: Could the precise architectural form of your residence influence how much you participate in politics? A new study by MIT scholars finds this to be exactly the case—at least in Accra, Ghana, where many people live in semi-communal structures known as "compound houses," often sharing kitchens, bathrooms and common living-room spaces, while having private bedrooms.

science.title: Researchers develop a new prodrug and localized drug delivery platform for selective treatment of cancer
science.summary: A new collaborative study reports the discovery and application of a novel therapeutic strategy to selectively target EGFR and other kinases with controlled release in tumor microenvironments to improve therapeutic efficacy, with promising results. The research is published in Bioorganic Chemistry.

science.title: This single well-known and widespread butterfly is actually three species in disguise
science.summary: The tropical rainforests of Central and South America are among the most biodiverse places on Earth. Costa Rica alone is home to half a million species, five times more than exist in the entire country of Canada, despite the former having 200 times less land area than the latter.

science.title: Tiny water droplets transmutate aniline into pyridine in ambient and catalyst-free conditions
science.summary: Aniline can now be transformed into pyridine without adding any catalysts, oxidants or toxic reagents. In a recent study published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society, researchers achieved skeletal editing, involving the reorganization of the carbon-nitrogen bonds within an aromatic ring, by turning an aqueous solution of aniline into a mist of microdroplets.

science.title: Discovery of enzymes that control pores on leaf surfaces could lead to drought-resistant crops
science.summary: A research team at the Ruhr University Bochum Department of Molecular and Cellular Botany, led by Professor Christopher Grefen, has uncovered how plants form the tiny pores on their leaves responsible for gas exchange and water regulation. The scientists identified the two lipid-modifying enzymes GELP80 and GELP100 as deciding factors in the formation of functional stomata.

science.title: After 70 years of excavation, ancient Sardis becomes a UNESCO World Heritage site
science.summary: After nearly seven decades of excavation, the legendary ancient city of Sardis has become a UNESCO World Heritage Site, celebrating years of discoveries that continue to reshape its history. Archaeologists say the biggest breakthroughs don't happen in a single season—they emerge as decades of evidence slowly come together.

science.title: NASA’s Lucy finds a wobbling peanut-shaped asteroid with signs of ancient water
science.summary: NASA’s Lucy spacecraft discovered that asteroid Donaldjohanson is a wobbling, peanut-shaped relic born from a violent collision and slowly reshaped by the subtle force of sunlight. It also carries traces of ancient water, making it an important clue to the solar system’s mysterious past.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Au împreună 316 ani și i-au uimit pe cercetători. Secretul a trei surori din Brazilia care au trecut de 100 de ani
mix_items.summary: Trei surori din Brazilia, care au împreună 316 ani și au fost desemnate recent de Guinness World Records drept cel mai vârstnic trio de surori din lume, se află în atenția cercetătorilor care studiază mecanismele longevității. Oamenii de știință speră că povestea lor de viață și factorii genetici ar putea oferi indicii importante despre secretul unei vieți de peste un secol, relatează Reuters.

mix_items.title: Filmul De capul nostru, regizat de Tudor Cristian Jurgiu și premiat la Berlinale, intră în cinematografele din România din 26 iunie
mix_items.summary: Transilvania Film anunță lansarea filmului De capul nostru, cel mai recent lungmetraj semnat de Tudor Cristian Jurgiu și premiat cu Premiul CICAE în secțiunea Forum a Festivalului Internațional de Film… Articolul Filmul De capul nostru, regizat de Tudor Cristian Jurgiu și premiat la Berlinale, intră în cinematografele din România din 26 iunie apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Simţi nevoia de senzaţii mai tari în cele 10 minute de pauză de prânz de la corporaţie? Luca are veşti bune pentru tine! mix_items.summary: București, 25 iunie 2026 – La începutul acestui an, YOLO Events și Ajungem MARI au lansat Fondul pentru Sport și Mișcare, un proiect prin care participarea la cursele Yolo Kids… Articolul De la linia de start la șanse reale pentru copii: Yolo Events și Ajungem MARI dezvoltă Fondul pentru Sport și Mișcare apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: „Străzi Deschise – București, Promenadă urbană” revine duminică, 28 iunie, cu un nou program dedicat redescoperirii orașului prin patrimoniu, dialog și implicare civică. mix_items.summary: Compania mizează pe sustenabilitate prin prelungirea duratei de viață a echipamentelor de top și servicii premium de reparații oferit clienților săi BUCUREȘTI, 25 iunie 2026 – Samsung Electronics România aduce… Articolul Samsung schimbă regulile în service: reparații rapide pentru telefoane și asistență la domiciliu pentru ecrane mari apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Primul studiu cu adevărat serios despre comportamentul urșilor din Munții Carpați a scos la iveală un detaliu extrem de interesant. Numărul tot mai mare de urși care coboară din pădure spre sate și chiar spre orașe nu are nicio legătură cu nevoia de hrană, ci cu… disperarea. Urșii efectiv se refugiază în zone locuite ca […] The post Studiu: 8 din 10 urşi coboară în oraşe ca să scape de cocalarii cu ATV-uri din pădure appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.summary: București, 20 mai 2026: Fundația World Vision România, împreună cu Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași, a inaugurat o seră... Articolul World Vision România investește în formarea viitorilor specialiști ai agriculturii românești. O seră didactică modernizată cu tehnologii de Agricultură 4.0, inaugurată la Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: AI framework helps identify new targets for CAR T cell therapy
mix_items.summary: Leading CAR T cell therapy researchers have developed a human-in-the-loop artificial intelligence (AI) framework that centers scientists' expertise to find viable target antigens for CAR T cell therapy. The work was led by experts from the Perelman School of Medicine at the University of Pennsylvania and Penn's Abramson Cancer Center and has been published in Cell.

mix_items.title: Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor
mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.summary: În România, mulți copii încă se tem să vorbească despre abuzurile prin care trec. Conform unui studiu al Fundației World... Articolul Doar 1 din 2 copii ar anunța poliția în cazul unui abuz. World Vision România: Copiii care raportează trebuie sprijiniți și încurajați, nu umiliți public. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Artificial 'leaf' powers wireless biomedical device
mix_items.summary: Plants convert light into energy efficiently through photosynthesis—an ability that scientists and engineers still struggle to match with electronic devices. Recently, researchers have looked beyond traditional semiconductor materials to create devices using a promising class of materials called nanoplasmonics. These tiny metal structures can absorb and concentrate optical energy and generate energetic charge carriers.

mix_items.title: Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor
mix_items.summary: Alexandra, adolescentă sprijinită de Fundația Inocenți, și-a recăpătat încrederea în sine și motivația pentru învățare prin suport educațional și emoțional. The post Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.summary: Locație: București (Satul SOS) Nivel carieră: Mid-level (3-5 ani experiență) Departament: Financiar / Contabilitate Tip job: Full-time Cine suntem noi? mix_items.title: New study calls for rethink of domestic abuse responses in children's services
mix_items.summary: A major new study has raised concerns about how domestic violence and abuse is understood and responded to within children's services.

mix_items.title: World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România
mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... Articolul World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Heat-struck Italians cool off in ancient stone 'trulli'
mix_items.summary: Once relics of a bygone way of life, the iconic dry-stone dwellings with conical roofs dotted across Puglia are in increasing demand as cool havens in Italy's sweltering south.

mix_items.title: Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat
mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional
mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Hybrid reef project off Miami Beach targets wave attenuation and coral recovery
mix_items.summary: University of Miami researchers and collaborators have expanded an offshore reef restoration and coastal resilience project off Miami Beach with the deployment of three 3D-printed SEAHIVE clustered structures.

mix_items.title: Language models read doctors' notes to reveal why patients discontinue medication
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University of Tartu showed that large language models can identify with high accuracy why patients stop using antidiabetic medications or statins based on doctors' electronic clinical notes. The study opens new possibilities for using clinical information that has so far been difficult to analyze in health research.

mix_items.title: Sponges may cut methylmercury contamination in marine food webs by more than 50%
mix_items.summary: Marine sponges may play an important, previously underestimated role in reducing methylmercury contamination in marine food webs. In a new modeling study, researchers at Helmholtz-Zentrum Hereon showed that sponges can significantly influence the spread of this environmental toxin through ecosystems. The findings suggest that the animals' unique feeding behavior helps reduce methylmercury levels and may lower contamination in fish. The study is published in the journal Biogeosciences.

mix_items.title: Hidden thymus step may prime cancer-killing T cells earlier than expected
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Mayo Clinic have uncovered a previously hidden step in how the immune system prepares to fight cancer, a discovery that could help scientists develop more effective and longer-lasting cancer immunotherapies.

mix_items.title: Use of supplement before surgery may improve liver regeneration
mix_items.summary: Taking a supplement before a hepatectomy, a surgery in which part of the liver is removed, may help the organ recover more quickly and become more resistant to further damage. This is the main finding of a study conducted by researchers at the State University of Campinas (UNICAMP) in the state of São Paulo, Brazil. The researchers investigated the effects of beta-hydroxy-beta-methylbutyrate (HMB) in an experimental model using mice. The results indicate that preoperative use of the supplement preserves the energy function of liver cells and improves the quality of regeneration following major injury. These results were published in March in the journal Acta Physiologica.

mix_items.title: Not everyone has equal access to pain rehabilitation
mix_items.summary: Chronic pain can affect work, everyday life and quality of life. Patients with chronic pain do not have access to specialized rehabilitation on equal terms. Education, age, sex and region of birth appear to play a role, particularly in combination, according to a study from the University of Gothenburg published in the Scandinavian Journal of Pain.

mix_items.title: Study explores environmental cost, perceptions of influencer PR gifts
mix_items.summary: As part of their marketing strategy, many companies send public relations packages full of curated products to social media influencers, who film themselves opening the gifts on platforms like Instagram and TikTok.

mix_items.title: Developing a new methodology to identify biomarkers for male infertility
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the EHU-University of the Basque Country have developed a robust protocol to detect the molecules involved in the metabolic reactions of sperm. The new method enables a detailed analysis even with small samples and allows the detection of the largest number of potential biomarkers identified to date; this advance could help diagnose male infertility and develop future clinical solutions. The paper is published in the journal Cells.

mix_items.title: Taking a step back changes group discussions in virtual environments
mix_items.summary: In group decision-making, it is critical for each member to engage in discussions with a broader perspective and without fixating on personal values and knowledge. While self-distancing, or stepping back and viewing oneself from a third-person perspective, has been associated with more objective thinking, its role in multiparty decision-making within virtual environments remains underexplored.

mix_items.title: First use of precision editing to study human embryo development reveals role of master gene
mix_items.summary: Research led by the University of Cambridge Loke Center for Trophoblast Research has shown that a genome-editing technique can be used to alter a single gene in human embryonic cells, enabling the study of very early human development in unparalleled detail. The study is published in the journal Nature.

mix_items.title: This common vitamin deficiency can mimic normal aging
mix_items.summary: Vitamin B12 is needed in microscopic amounts, but a shortage can have major effects on health and energy. The vitamin was first linked to a lifesaving liver treatment for pernicious anemia nearly 100 years ago. Today, researchers are finding that B12 may also help keep cellular powerhouses called mitochondria functioning properly. This could explain why some people experience fatigue and brain fog even before traditional signs of deficiency show up.

mix_items.title: Trios of quantum particles form checkerboard layouts when particle density hits sweet spot
mix_items.summary: Trions form when three particles, like quarks or electrons, come together. This formation occurs in quantum particles in nuclear physics, semiconductors and magnets, and understanding its behavior can be challenging. Rice University's Kaden Hazzard and his team recently developed a theory on how these formations occur and behave, which was published in Physical Review Letters.

mix_items.title: Researchers identify dual-function rice gene that boosts drought tolerance and grain yield
mix_items.summary: As climate change intensifies droughts and other environmental stresses, maintaining crop productivity has become a major challenge for global agriculture. Drought can impair chloroplast development, reducing photosynthetic efficiency and ultimately lowering crop yields.

mix_items.title: What really controls water chemistry in nanoscale spaces
mix_items.summary: Water is the most studied molecule on Earth, yet a surprisingly basic question has gone unanswered for decades: When water is squeezed into gaps just a few molecules wide—as happens inside nanoscale pores, membranes and biological channels—does it become more or less chemically reactive?

mix_items.title: More can be done to reduce Legionnaires' disease risk in Australia, according to study
mix_items.summary: Flinders University researchers have identified opportunities to strengthen Legionella risk management across Australia by introducing uniform best practices across all states and territories.

mix_items.title: Poor metabolic health can age the brain even in young people, finds new large-scale study
mix_items.summary: Two people of very different ages can have a similar level of biological aging in their brains. Such an occurrence is possible because aging and metabolic health follow two distinct pathways that influence brain health. While it is known that the brain changes as we get older, a recent study analyzing more than 3,000 brain scans found that metabolic issues affect the brain through a different biological pathway than aging does.

mix_items.title: Study analyzes how genes and education influence socioeconomic success
mix_items.summary: Ambitious public education policies have the capacity to cushion the role of the "genetic lottery" in people's life chances. This is one of the main conclusions of a scientific study by Universidad Carlos III de Madrid (UC3M), the University of Lausanne (Switzerland) and Stockholm University (Sweden) analyzing the impact of an educational reform in England. These findings could be applied to the design of future educational policies.

mix_items.title: Thirsty desert lizards inspire a new water-harvesting system
mix_items.summary: When the desert horned lizard (Phrynosoma platyrhinos) is thirsty, it cannot just lap up water or scoop it up like a bird because it lives in environments where water is extremely scarce. Typically, it's found in damp soil or, even more rarely, in drops of rain.

mix_items.title: Radioligand therapy for HER2-positive cancers enters human trials for the first time
mix_items.summary: The McGill University Health Centre (MUHC) is the first site in the world to launch a first-in-human phase 1 clinical trial evaluating a novel radioligand therapy for HER2-positive cancers, including certain types of breast cancer.

mix_items.title: Megacluster of bacterial genes reveals four antibiotics that jointly starve rivals of biotin
mix_items.summary: Researchers at McMaster University have discovered what they describe as a "megacluster" of genes in Streptomyces bacteria that produces four antibiotics that work together to stop rival bacteria.

mix_items.title: Semi-communal 'compound houses' in Ghana affect how people participate in political activity
mix_items.summary: Could the precise architectural form of your residence influence how much you participate in politics? A new study by MIT scholars finds this to be exactly the case—at least in Accra, Ghana, where many people live in semi-communal structures known as "compound houses," often sharing kitchens, bathrooms and common living-room spaces, while having private bedrooms.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover how a single cell builds a brain with 170 billion cells
mix_items.summary: How does a single cell build a brain with billions of precisely organized neurons? Researchers suggest that brain cells use their lineage—their cellular family tree—as a kind of positional map. Cells that come from the same ancestor stay near one another, helping the brain organize itself without relying solely on chemical signals.

mix_items.title: Researchers develop a new prodrug and localized drug delivery platform for selective treatment of cancer
mix_items.summary: A new collaborative study reports the discovery and application of a novel therapeutic strategy to selectively target EGFR and other kinases with controlled release in tumor microenvironments to improve therapeutic efficacy, with promising results. The research is published in Bioorganic Chemistry.

mix_items.title: This single well-known and widespread butterfly is actually three species in disguise
mix_items.summary: The tropical rainforests of Central and South America are among the most biodiverse places on Earth. Costa Rica alone is home to half a million species, five times more than exist in the entire country of Canada, despite the former having 200 times less land area than the latter.

mix_items.title: Tiny water droplets transmutate aniline into pyridine in ambient and catalyst-free conditions
mix_items.summary: Aniline can now be transformed into pyridine without adding any catalysts, oxidants or toxic reagents. In a recent study published in the Journal of the American Chemical Society, researchers achieved skeletal editing, involving the reorganization of the carbon-nitrogen bonds within an aromatic ring, by turning an aqueous solution of aniline into a mist of microdroplets.

mix_items.title: Targeting cannabinoid receptors in bladder sensory pathways may alleviate pain of cystitis
mix_items.summary: The burning pain associated with cystitis affects millions of people around the world, and it can take days or even years for some patients to find relief from bladder pain, infection-related symptoms and the constant need to urinate. A new study led by Flinders University published in Autonomic Neuroscience, has shown how certain pain receptors in the bladder could be targeted to reduce the debilitating effects of this common condition.

mix_items.title: Discovery of enzymes that control pores on leaf surfaces could lead to drought-resistant crops
mix_items.summary: A research team at the Ruhr University Bochum Department of Molecular and Cellular Botany, led by Professor Christopher Grefen, has uncovered how plants form the tiny pores on their leaves responsible for gas exchange and water regulation. The scientists identified the two lipid-modifying enzymes GELP80 and GELP100 as deciding factors in the formation of functional stomata.

mix_items.title: Unfolding stomach capsule could target ulcer-causing bacteria for 48 hours
mix_items.summary: Between 50% and 100% of people in countries with poor access to clean water are infected with a bacterium called Helicobacter pylori. This bacterium is a leading cause of stomach ulcers, and it can even cause gastric cancer.

mix_items.title: They knew the pill was fake but their memory still improved
mix_items.summary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.

mix_items.title: After 70 years of excavation, ancient Sardis becomes a UNESCO World Heritage site
mix_items.summary: After nearly seven decades of excavation, the legendary ancient city of Sardis has become a UNESCO World Heritage Site, celebrating years of discoveries that continue to reshape its history. Archaeologists say the biggest breakthroughs don't happen in a single season—they emerge as decades of evidence slowly come together.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Lucy finds a wobbling peanut-shaped asteroid with signs of ancient water
mix_items.summary: NASA’s Lucy spacecraft discovered that asteroid Donaldjohanson is a wobbling, peanut-shaped relic born from a violent collision and slowly reshaped by the subtle force of sunlight. It also carries traces of ancient water, making it an important clue to the solar system’s mysterious past.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am organizat biroul pe categorii: urgente, importante si maine.

satire_ro.title: Încă un turist muşcat de peştele-iepure după ce i-a ieşit morcovul din slip
